title: Hypothesis Testing & Introduction to ANOVA
summary: Lecture 8. Normality tests *warn* that a systematic factor is present; to find out **which factor** causes it, we put the suspected factor under control and test it. You will learn the logic of a **hypothesis test** — null vs alternative, the significance level $\alpha$, type-I/II errors, one- vs two-tailed, the test statistic and the critical value / p-value — the **t-test** on a mean and on two means, and then the **analysis of variance (ANOVA)**: comparing the *between-group* variance with the *within-group* variance through $F=MS_{between}/MS_{within}$ and deciding with the Fisher $F$-test.
objectives: State a null hypothesis $H_0$ (no systematic effect) and its alternative, and explain why we 'reject' or 'fail to reject' but never 'accept' $H_0$.
Define the significance level / risk of error $\alpha$ and relate type-I and type-II errors to the consequences of a wrong decision.
Build the non-rejection (confidence) interval and decide one- vs two-tailed; reach the same decision via the test statistic, critical value, or p-value.
Run a z/t-test on a single mean and a t-test comparing two means (pairwise, non-overlapping confidence intervals).
Decompose total variability into between-group ($SS_B$) and within-group ($SS_W$) sums of squares for a single factor.
Compute the ANOVA variance ratio $F=s_B^2/s_W^2$ and decide with the upper Fisher limit $F_U$ (or the F p-value).
Explain what ANOVA can and cannot tell you (it flags the factor, not the element; it can miss a slow drift).

blocks.text: The $\chi^2$ test and the NPP detect a *significant distortion* of the data distribution — a **warning** that systematic factors exist. To connect that warning to a **cause**, we put the **suspected factor under control**: divide the data into groups that are homogeneous with respect to that factor (e.g. one group per operator) and test whether the differences between groups are **systematic** or **just random**. Investigate only when there is concrete evidence of significance — chasing purely random differences is wasteful.

The **null hypothesis** $H_0$ is formulated in the **opposite direction** to the presence of the factor under control — i.e. *'there is no significant effect / no difference'*. The **alternative** is that the factor does have an effect. We test $H_0$ and decide whether the data give us reason to **reject** it.

blocks.title: You never 'accept' the null
blocks.text: A hypothesis test will **never accept** the hypothesis of non-significance. Either we **reject** $H_0$ (at the chosen risk), or we find **no reason to reject** it — which is not proof that the factor is absent, only that the data do not reveal it. 'Fail to reject' ≠ 'accept'.

blocks.term: Risk of error α, type I and type II
blocks.text: The **risk of the first kind** $\alpha$ is the probability of **rejecting $H_0$ when it is true** (a false alarm). The confidence level is $P=1-\alpha$. A **type-II error** is *failing to reject $H_0$ when it is false* (missing a real effect). Lowering $\alpha$ guards against false alarms but raises the chance of missing real effects, so $\alpha$ is chosen by weighing the **consequences** of each error.

blocks.title: Why α = 20% here
blocks.text: In this course the operator/ANOVA tests use a deliberately **high** $\alpha=20\%$ ($P=80\%$). Re-checking an operator's procedure is **cheap**, so a high first-kind risk (false alarm) is acceptable, and it **lowers the second-kind risk** of missing a genuine systematic effect. The right $\alpha$ always follows from the cost of the two errors.

blocks.title: The four steps of a hypothesis test

steps.label: a
steps.text: Represent the question analytically and decide how to **group** the data to isolate the suspected factor.

steps.label: b
steps.text: Choose the **statistical distribution** assumed to describe the analyzed quantity (normal for a mean of many results, Student-$t$ for a small sample, Fisher-$F$ for a variance ratio).

steps.label: c
steps.text: Fix the acceptable **risk of the first kind $\alpha$** from the consequences of a false alarm.

steps.label: d
steps.text: Determine the **boundaries of the non-rejection zone** — once the distribution and confidence level are known, these are the limits of the relevant **confidence interval**. Reject $H_0$ if the test statistic falls outside.

blocks.term: One-tailed vs two-tailed; statistic, critical value, p-value
blocks.text: A test is **two-tailed** when a deviation in *either* direction matters (e.g. a mean different from target — split $\alpha$ as $\alpha/2$ per tail). It is **one-tailed** when only one direction is meaningful (e.g. an ANOVA variance ratio, which can only be inflated by a real effect). The decision can be read three equivalent ways: compare the **test statistic** with the **critical value**, check whether the statistic lies inside the **confidence interval**, or compare the **p-value** with $\alpha$ (reject if p $<\alpha$).
3. The hypothesis test for the mean

Question 1 — *are the operators homogeneous?* The reference distribution is the distribution of the **averages of $q$ results**: a normal with mean $m$ (the overall average) and standard deviation $s_m$, the standard deviation of an average of $q$ data, obtained from the standard deviation $s$ of all data.

blocks.title: Standard deviation of the mean; z-test limits
blocks.tex: s_m=\frac{s}{\sqrt{q}},\qquad \text{confidence limits}=m\mp z\,s_m,\quad z=\text{value at cumulative }1-\tfrac{\alpha}{2}
blocks.caption: Each operator's average is compared with the band $m\mp z\,s_m$. Any operator average **outside** the band signals a systematic difference. With $m=8.0062$, $s_m=0.0009$ mm, $P=80\%$ the limits are $8.0051$/$8.0073$ mm; operators 1, 2, 4, 5 fall outside, so the null of homogeneity is **rejected** at $\alpha=20\%$.

blocks.title: Difference between two means
blocks.caption: Question 2 — *are two operators' averages different?* The reference is a normal with **zero** mean (the null says the difference is not systematic) and standard deviation $s_d$. If the observed $d$ falls outside $[\,-z\,s_d,\;+z\,s_d\,]$, the difference is systematic.

blocks.title: z-test vs t-test (the logical bug)
blocks.text: The **z-test** above uses the standard deviation $s$ of **all** the data — which is itself inflated by the very systematic effects we are hunting, so the test may **fail to detect** them. The fix is the **t-test**: compute a confidence interval for **each sample's average** using a **Student-$t$** distribution, then compare the intervals **pairwise** — if two intervals do **not overlap**, a systematic difference is flagged. With the t-test there is **no single common reference**.

blocks.title: Student-t distribution explorer
blocks.caption: With small samples the $t$ distribution is fatter-tailed than the normal, so its critical values are larger. Lower the degrees of freedom and watch the tails thicken and the two-sided critical $t$ grow — this is why a small-sample mean test must use $t$, not $z$.

blocks.title: Design the experiment to separate effects
blocks.text: A drift (from regression) and an operator effect can be **superimposed**, because operators took data in succession. You cannot disentangle them *after* the fact. Plan the data collection so the two components are independent — e.g. take readings **symmetrically about the centre** of the sequence. In mass metrology the **ABBA scheme** (standard A, item B, item B, standard A) cancels a linear drift by averaging.
4. The hypothesis test for the variance

Differences may also appear in the **spread**. The dispersion homogeneity of samples is tested through Pearson's **$\chi^2$**, which describes the distribution of the estimator $s^2$ of the variance $\sigma^2$ of a normally distributed population. The sample variance is a correct estimator of the population variance.

blocks.title: Pearson chi-square for a sample variance
blocks.caption: Each operator's $q$ readings have mean $m_i$ and variance $s_i^2$; the whole set has variance $\sigma^2$. The confidence interval for $s_i^2$ at level $P$ ($\alpha=1-P$) comes from this $\chi^2_{(q-1)}$ distribution — used to check whether the operators' spreads are homogeneous.

blocks.title: Chi-square distribution explorer
blocks.caption: The $\chi^2$ distribution of a sample variance has $q-1$ degrees of freedom and is right-skewed. Vary the dof to see how the two-sided acceptance band for a variance widens for small samples — the basis of the variance homogeneity test.

blocks.text: **ANOVA** is a test based on the **ratio of two different estimates of the experimental variance**, arranged so the **denominator does not contain** the systematic effect of the factor under control, while the **numerator may** be inflated by it. Its **disadvantage**: it flags the *factor* responsible, not the *specific element* of the factor. Its **advantages**: it can handle **more than one factor at once**, and its reference (the within-group variance) is **not influenced** by the systematic factors being analyzed.

blocks.title: Variance between groups
blocks.caption: The **between-groups** variance measures how far the $k$ group means $\bar x_{\bullet j}$ scatter around the grand mean $\bar x_{\bullet\bullet}$ ($n$ = readings per group). $SS_B$ is the *sum of squares between groups*; its degrees of freedom are $k-1$. It is **inflated** if the factor (operator) has a real effect.

blocks.title: Variance within groups
blocks.caption: The **within-groups** variance averages the scatter of each datum about *its own* group mean. It captures **only random effects** — it does not see the between-group differences — so it is the clean reference. Degrees of freedom $k(n-1)$. The total decomposes as $SS_{TC}=SS_W+SS_B$.

blocks.title: The Fisher F-test
Fisher F distribution explorer
blocks.caption: Set the numerator dof $\nu_1=k-1$ and denominator dof $\nu_2=k(n-1)$ to see the right-tail critical $F_U$. ANOVA rejects equal means only when $F_{exp}$ lands in that right tail — the variance ratio can only be *inflated* by a real effect, hence a one-sided comparison.

blocks.title: Worked: operator effect (single factor)
blocks.text:
Five operators ($k=5$), ten readings each ($n=10$, $N=50$). The ANOVA table gives $SS_B=1.32\times10^{-4}$ on $\nu_1=k-1=4$ dof $\Rightarrow s_B^2=3.31\times10^{-5}$; $SS_W=2.33\times10^{-4}$ on $\nu_2=k(n-1)=45$ dof $\Rightarrow s_W^2=5.17\times10^{-6}$.

$$F_{exp}=\frac{s_B^2}{s_W^2}=\frac{3.31\times10^{-5}}{5.17\times10^{-6}}=6.4.$$
The upper Fisher limit at $\alpha=20\%$ is $F_U=1.6$. Since $F_{exp}=6.4>F_U=1.6$, the variance ratio is **outside** the interval: the differences between operator averages are **systematic** with a 20% risk of error.

blocks.title: Two factors at once
blocks.text: Organize the data so rows and columns each carry a factor (e.g. **operator** across columns, **replication / sequence** down rows) and ANOVA tests both **simultaneously**: $SS_{TC}=SS_{Bc}+SS_{Br}+SS_W$, with a separate $F$ (and $F_U$) for columns and for rows. In the case study the operator factor came out systematic ($F=6.92>F_U$), while nothing could be concluded about the row (sequence) factor.

blocks.title: ANOVA can miss a drift
blocks.text: Regression found a clear drift, but ANOVA on the **rows** did not flag it. Why? ANOVA compares group means over **short intervals**, while a drift accumulates over the **whole** sequence — segmented into rows, the slow trend almost cancels. Always read statistical results **critically**: regression catches drifts, ANOVA catches group differences; you need both, and you must plan the experiment to keep the factors separable.

question.prompt: In a one-way ANOVA the variance ratio is $F_{exp}=6.4$ and the upper Fisher limit at the chosen confidence is $F_U=1.6$. The correct conclusion about the operators is:

options.content: $F_{exp}>F_U$, so the differences between operators are systematic — reject equal means
$F_{exp}>F_U$, so the operators are equivalent
$F_{exp}<F_U$, so there is no operator effect
No conclusion is possible without the regression slope

question.explanation: $F=s_B^2/s_W^2=6.4$ far exceeds the upper limit $F_U=1.6$: the between-group variance dominates the random within-group variance, so the null of equal means is rejected — the operator effect is **systematic** (at the chosen risk). **B** has the right inequality but the wrong meaning. **C** reverses the inequality ($6.4>1.6$). **D** is irrelevant — ANOVA needs no regression. The test is one-sided: only the upper limit matters because a real effect can only inflate $F$.
question.theory: ANOVA: reject equal means when $F_{exp}>F_U=\mathrm{F.INV.RT}(\alpha;\nu_1;\nu_2)$; the F-test is one-tailed.

practice.prompt: In a hypothesis test, the null hypothesis $H_0$ is formulated:

options.content: in the same direction as the suspected factor (the factor is present)
in the opposite direction to the factor under control (no significant effect / no difference)
so that it can always be accepted
only after the p-value is known

practice.explanation: $H_0$ is stated in the **opposite direction** to the presence of the factor: *'there is no significant effect.'* We then look for evidence to reject it. **A** describes the *alternative* hypothesis. **C** is wrong — a hypothesis test never *accepts* the null; it only fails to reject it. **D** is backwards: the hypothesis is set before computing any statistic or p-value.
practice.theory: The null is the 'no-effect' statement; testing seeks grounds to reject it, never to accept it.

practice.prompt: The **risk of the first kind** $\alpha$ in a hypothesis test is the probability of:

options.content: failing to reject $H_0$ when it is false (missing a real effect)
the data being non-normal
rejecting $H_0$ when it is actually true (a false alarm)
choosing the wrong statistical distribution

practice.explanation: The first-kind (type-I) error is **rejecting a true null** — flagging an effect that is not there — and its probability is $\alpha$ (with $P=1-\alpha$). **A** describes the *second-kind* (type-II) error. **B** and **D** are unrelated to the definition of $\alpha$. Choosing $\alpha$ trades off false alarms against missed effects.
practice.theory: Type I: reject true $H_0$, probability $\alpha$. Type II: fail to reject false $H_0$. Lowering $\alpha$ raises type-II risk.

practice.prompt: Why does the course adopt a relatively high $\alpha=20\%$ for the operator/ANOVA tests?

options.content: Because re-checking an operator's procedure is cheap, so a high false-alarm risk is acceptable and it lowers the chance of missing a real effect
Because a high $\alpha$ makes the test impossible to fail
Because $\alpha=20\%$ is mandatory for all $\chi^2$ tests
Because it guarantees the data are normal

practice.explanation: $\alpha$ is set from the **consequences** of the two errors. Re-examining an operator is inexpensive, so tolerating more false alarms (high $\alpha$) is fine, and it **reduces the second-kind risk** of overlooking a genuine systematic effect. **B** misstates what $\alpha$ does. **C** is false — confidence levels are chosen case by case (the normality $\chi^2$ used $\alpha=15\%$). **D** confuses the significance level with a normality guarantee.
practice.theory: Choose $\alpha$ by weighing the cost of a false alarm against the cost of missing an effect.

practice.prompt: Comparing two operators' means, why is the **t-test** (pairwise non-overlapping confidence intervals) preferred over the **z-test**?

options.content: Because the t distribution has thinner tails than the normal
Because the t-test needs no degrees of freedom
Because the z-test cannot be computed in Excel
Because the z-test uses the standard deviation of all the data, which is inflated by the very systematic effects being sought, so it may fail to detect them

practice.explanation: The z-test's reference uses $s$ of **all** the data, which is itself blown up by the systematic effects under investigation — a logical bug that can hide those effects. The t-test instead builds a Student-$t$ confidence interval for **each** sample and compares them pairwise (non-overlap $\Rightarrow$ systematic difference). **A** is false: the $t$ has *fatter* tails than the normal, especially for small samples. **B** is wrong — the $t$ depends on degrees of freedom. **C** is false (T.INV.2T etc. exist).
practice.theory: z-test common reference is contaminated by systematic effects; per-sample t-intervals avoid it. The $t$ is fatter-tailed for small $n$.

practice.prompt: In one-way ANOVA, which variance serves as the *clean reference* (the denominator of $F$), and why?

options.content: The between-groups variance $s_B^2$, because it contains the factor effect
The within-groups variance $s_W^2$, because it captures only random effects and not the between-group differences
The total variance, because it is the largest
Either one; the choice does not matter

practice.explanation: The **within-groups** variance averages each datum's scatter about *its own* group mean, so it sees only random noise and is **not influenced** by the factor under control — the ideal denominator. The **between-groups** variance $s_B^2$ goes in the **numerator** because it *is* inflated by a real factor effect. **A** swaps the roles. **C** would mix systematic and random parts. **D** is wrong — the whole design depends on a denominator free of the factor.
practice.theory: $F=s_B^2/s_W^2$: numerator may carry the effect, denominator (within) carries only random variation.

practice.prompt: For a single-factor ANOVA with $k$ groups and $n$ readings per group, the degrees of freedom of the between-groups and within-groups variances are:

options.content: $n-1$ (between) and $k-1$ (within)
$k$ (between) and $kn$ (within)
$k-1$ (between) and $k(n-1)$ (within)
$kn-1$ (between) and $1$ (within)

practice.explanation:
Between-groups compares $k$ group means about the grand mean: $
u_1=k-1$. Within-groups pools $k$ estimates each on $n-1$ dof: $
u_2=k(n-1)$. They sum to the total $kn-1$. For $k=5$, $n=10$: $
u_1=4$, $
u_2=45$ — exactly the case-study table. **A** swaps and mislabels them. **B** forgets the $-1$ corrections. **D** is the total-vs-nothing split, not the ANOVA decomposition.

practice.prompt: Why is the ANOVA F-test **one-tailed** (only the upper Fisher limit $F_U$ matters)?

options.content: Because $F$ can be negative, so only the upper side is defined
Because the lower Fisher limit is always zero
Because the normality test is also one-tailed
Because a real systematic effect can only inflate the numerator, making $F>1$; a small $F$ never indicates an effect

practice.explanation: If the factor has no effect, $s_B^2$ and $s_W^2$ both estimate $\sigma^2$ and $F\approx1$; a genuine effect inflates **only the numerator** $s_B^2$, pushing $F$ above 1. So evidence of an effect lives entirely in the **upper tail**, and we compare $F_{exp}$ only with $F_U$. **A** is false — $F$ is a ratio of variances, never negative. **B** is irrelevant to the decision. **C** is wrong: the normality $\chi^2$ is two-sided.
practice.theory: The variance ratio is polarized: the systematic effect makes the expected ratio $>1$, so the test is upper-tailed.

practice.prompt: Regression clearly revealed a drift in the data, yet the two-factor ANOVA did not flag the row (sequence) factor. The best explanation is:

options.content: ANOVA compares group means over short intervals, so a slow drift accumulating over the whole sequence is segmented and largely cancels
ANOVA is simply wrong and should be ignored
The drift was actually a random effect
Regression and ANOVA must always agree, so one result is a calculation error

practice.explanation: A drift builds up across the **entire** sequence, but ANOVA looks at differences between rows over **much shorter** intervals, so the slow trend is split into pieces that nearly cancel — ANOVA does not see it. Regression, which fits the whole sequence, does. **B** is wrong — both methods are valid for what they measure. **C** contradicts the regression evidence of a systematic trend. **D** is false: the two tools answer different questions (drift vs group differences) and need not agree.
practice.theory: Regression detects continuous drifts; ANOVA detects between-group differences. Read each result critically — they are complementary.

exam.title: One-way ANOVA: build the F-test and decide
exam.statement: Five operators ($k=5$) each take $n=10$ readings ($N=50$). The sums of squares are $SS_B=1.32\times10^{-4}$ (between operators) and $SS_W=2.33\times10^{-4}$ (within). At $\alpha=20\%$ the upper Fisher limit is $F_U=1.6$. (a) Give the degrees of freedom. (b) Compute $s_B^2$, $s_W^2$ and $F_{exp}$. (c) Decide whether the operator differences are systematic, and state the risk.

steps.title: Decision (one-tailed)
steps.content: Compare only with the upper limit: $F_{exp}=6.4>F_U=1.6$, so $F_{exp}$ is outside the confidence interval. **Reject** the null of equal means: the differences between operator averages are **systematic** with a risk of error of $20\%$. Equivalently the p-value F.DIST.RT$(6.4;4;45)\ll0.20$.

exam.title: Hypothesis test on a mean (z-test band)
exam.meta: Lecture 8 · test for the mean · numeric/conceptual
exam.statement: Five operators each report the average of $q=10$ readings. The overall average is $m=8.0062$ mm and the standard deviation of an operator's average is $s_m=0.0009$ mm. Test, at confidence $P=80\%$ (so $z\approx1.28$ for $1-\alpha/2=0.90$), whether the operators are homogeneous, given the operator averages $m_1=8.0048$, $m_2=8.0047$, $m_3=8.0051$, $m_4=8.0085$, $m_5=8.0079$ mm. (a) Build the confidence band. (b) Identify which operators fall outside. (c) State the conclusion and one caveat of this z-test.

steps.title: Confidence band
steps.content: Limits $m\mp z\,s_m=8.0062\mp1.28\times0.0009=8.0062\mp0.00115$, i.e. about $[\,8.0051,\;8.0073\,]$ mm (matching the course table).

steps.title: Place each operator
steps.content: $m_1=8.0048$ (below), $m_2=8.0047$ (below), $m_3=8.0051$ (on the lower edge), $m_4=8.0085$ (above), $m_5=8.0079$ (above). Operators 1, 2, 4 and 5 fall **outside** the band.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Because four of the five averages lie outside the non-rejection band, the null hypothesis that the operators are homogeneous is **rejected** at a risk of error of $20\%$: the differences among the operator means are **systematic**.

steps.title: Caveat (the logical bug)
steps.content: The band uses $s_m=s/\sqrt{q}$ built from the standard deviation of **all** the data, which is itself inflated by the systematic effects we are testing for. A cleaner approach is the **t-test**: per-operator Student-$t$ confidence intervals compared pairwise (non-overlap $\Rightarrow$ systematic difference).

exam.finalAnswer: Band $\approx[8.0051,\,8.0073]$ mm; operators 1, 2, 4, 5 outside $\Rightarrow$ reject homogeneity at $\alpha=20\%$ (systematic differences). Caveat: the z-test reference is contaminated by the systematic effects — use the t-test to overcome it.
exam.tips: $z\approx1.28$ corresponds to $1-\alpha/2=0.90$ for a two-sided $80\%$ test (not $1.96$, which is $95\%$). Mention the contaminated-reference bug and the t-test fix — examiners reward it.
